seoTitle: AcelaMicro Products | Data Converters, Power Management, Interface ICs | LiTong
seoDescription: Explore AcelaMicro's comprehensive product portfolio including precision data converters, power management ICs, and interface solutions for industrial and automotive applications.

faqs.question: What product categories does AcelaMicro offer?
faqs.answer: AcelaMicro offers four main product categories: Data Converters (ADCs and DACs), Power Management ICs (DC-DC converters, LDOs, battery management), Interface ICs (RS-485, CAN, isolated interfaces), and Sensor Interface solutions (signal conditioning, sensor front-ends). Each category is designed to meet the demanding requirements of industrial and automotive applications with high reliability and performance. Our data converters range from 12-bit to 24-bit resolution with sampling rates up to 1 MSPS. Power management solutions include high-efficiency DC-DC converters and ultra-low-noise LDOs. Interface products provide robust communication in noisy industrial environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our product categories to find the right solution for your application, or contact our FAE team for personalized recommendations based on your specific requirements.

faqs.question: How do I select the right AcelaMicro product for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right AcelaMicro product involves several key considerations. First, identify your application requirements including resolution, speed, power consumption, and operating temperature range. For data converters, consider sampling rate and accuracy requirements - use SAR ADCs for high-speed applications and sigma-delta ADCs for precision measurement. For power management, evaluate input/output voltage ranges, current requirements, and efficiency targets. For interface ICs, determine communication protocol, data rate, and isolation requirements. Consider environmental factors like temperature range and EMI conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review our detailed product specifications and application notes, or request a consultation with our FAE team for expert guidance on product selection.

faqs.question: Where can I purchase AcelaMicro products?
faqs.answer: AcelaMicro products are available through authorized distributors including LiTong Electronics. As an authorized distributor, LiTong provides genuine AcelaMicro products with full technical support, competitive pricing, and reliable supply chain services. We maintain inventory of popular products for quick delivery and can support high-volume production requirements with flexible scheduling. Contact our sales team for quotations, sample requests, and delivery schedules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong Electronics sales team for quotations and to verify our authorized distributor status.

faqs.question: What technical support is available for AcelaMicro products?
faqs.answer: LiTong Electronics provides comprehensive technical support for AcelaMicro products through our experienced FAE team. Support services include product selection guidance, application circuit design review, PCB layout recommendations, and debugging assistance. We offer reference designs, evaluation boards, and detailed application notes. Our FAEs have extensive experience in analog and mixed-signal design across industrial, automotive, and consumer applications. For complex projects, we can arrange direct support from AcelaMicro's engineering team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team early in your design phase for optimal product selection and application guidance.

faqs.question: Are AcelaMicro products qualified for automotive applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, many AcelaMicro products are AEC-Q100 qualified for automotive applications. These products undergo rigorous qualification testing including temperature cycling, high-temperature operating life (HTOL), and ESD testing. Automotive-grade products are available in data converters, power management, and interface categories. AcelaMicro holds IATF 16949 certification, demonstrating commitment to automotive quality standards. Our automotive products are used in EV battery management, motor control, ADAS sensors, and body electronics applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Look for AEC-Q100 qualification in product specifications for automotive applications. Contact us for PPAP documentation support.

categories.name: Data Converters
categories.description: AcelaMicro's data converter portfolio includes high-precision ADCs and DACs designed for demanding industrial and automotive applications.
categories.longDescription: AcelaMicro data converter distributor selection guide: Our comprehensive portfolio includes precision SAR ADCs (12-16 bit) for high-speed data acquisition, sigma-delta ADCs (16-24 bit) for precision measurement, and high-resolution DACs for control systems. The ACM series data converters feature industry-leading performance with sampling rates from 1 SPS to 1 MSPS, resolutions from 12-bit to 24-bit, and excellent noise performance. These products are ideal for industrial automation, medical instrumentation, test equipment, and automotive applications. All products are available through LiTong Electronics with full technical support and competitive pricing.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select AcelaMicro Data Converters
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting the right ADC or DAC based on resolution, speed, and application requirements.

faqs.question: How do I choose between successive approximation and sigma-delta ADCs?
faqs.answer: Successive approximation (SAR) ADCs from AcelaMicro offer high sampling rates with moderate resolution (12-16 bits), making them ideal for multiplexed sensor systems and control applications. SAR ADCs provide low latency and deterministic conversion times. Sigma-delta ADCs provide higher resolution (16-24 bits) at lower sampling rates, perfect for precision measurement applications like weigh scales and temperature sensors. They use oversampling and noise shaping to achieve excellent dynamic range. Choose SAR for speed, sigma-delta for precision.
faqs.decisionGuide: For multi-channel sensor monitoring and control loops, choose SAR ADCs. For high-precision single-channel measurements, select sigma-delta ADCs.

faqs.question: What resolution do I need for my application?
faqs.answer: Resolution requirements depend on your measurement accuracy needs. For temperature measurement with 0.1°C accuracy, you typically need 16+ bits. For weigh scales requiring 1:10,000 resolution, 24 bits is recommended. For battery monitoring with 1mV accuracy on a 5V scale, 12-14 bits is sufficient. For audio applications, 16-24 bits provides good dynamic range. Consider that effective resolution (ENOB) is typically 2-4 bits less than the ADC's nominal resolution due to noise. AcelaMicro's ACM1604 provides 16-bit resolution with approximately 14.5 ENOB at full speed, while the ACM2420 provides 24-bit resolution with 20 ENOB at low data rates.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required resolution based on measurement accuracy, then add 4 bits margin for ENOB. Contact our FAE for detailed calculations.

faqs.question: How do I interpret the specifications in the product table?
faqs.answer: The product table provides key specifications for comparing data converters. Resolution indicates the number of bits in the digital output. Sampling rate determines how fast the ADC can convert analog signals. SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) indicates dynamic performance - higher is better. INL (Integral Non-Linearity) and DNL (Differential Non-Linearity) measure static accuracy. Power consumption is critical for battery-operated devices. Interface type (SPI, I2C) affects connection to your microcontroller. Use these parameters to narrow down options, then review the detailed datasheet for complete specifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with resolution and sampling rate requirements, then filter by interface and power constraints. Download datasheets for detailed specifications.

faqs.question: What is the difference between single-ended and differential inputs?
faqs.answer: Single-ended inputs measure voltage between the input pin and ground, offering simpler design but susceptible to ground noise. Differential inputs measure the voltage difference between two input pins, rejecting common-mode noise that appears equally on both inputs. Differential inputs provide better noise immunity and are recommended for industrial environments, long cable runs, and small signal measurements. AcelaMicro ADCs like the ACM1604 and ACM2420 support differential inputs with excellent CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) of 120 dB, meaning common-mode noise is rejected by a factor of 1,000,000. Use differential inputs for noisy environments and precision measurements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use differential inputs for noisy industrial environments and precision measurements. Use single-ended for simple, cost-sensitive applications.

faqs.question: How do I interface AcelaMicro ADCs with my microcontroller?
faqs.answer: AcelaMicro ADCs support standard serial interfaces for easy microcontroller integration. The ACM1604 uses SPI interface with CS, SCLK, DIN, and DOUT pins, supporting clock rates up to 20 MHz. The ACM2420 also uses SPI with similar connectivity. Both support SPI modes 0 and 3. For I2C-compatible devices, check our interface ICs section. Connection is straightforward: connect CS to a GPIO for device selection, SCLK to the SPI clock, DIN to MOSI, and DOUT to MISO. Example code is available for Arduino, STM32, and other popular platforms. Our FAE team can provide reference schematics and code examples.
faqs.decisionGuide: Ensure your microcontroller supports SPI mode 0 or 3. Contact our FAE for reference code and connection diagrams.

products.name: 16-Bit 4-Channel SAR ADC
products.shortDescription: 16-bit SAR ADC with 4 differential channels, 1 MSPS sampling rate, and SPI interface for industrial applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACM1604 is a high-performance 16-bit successive approximation register (SAR) analog-to-digital converter featuring four differential input channels.
With a sampling rate of 1 MSPS and excellent DC accuracy (INL: +/- 1 LSB), this ADC is ideal for multi-channel sensor monitoring and industrial control systems.
The device operates from a single 5V supply and includes an internal reference, programmable gain amplifier, and robust ESD protection.

alternativeParts.reason: Similar 16-bit resolution with I2C interface, suitable for lower speed applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for low-speed, I2C-based systems where sampling rate is not critical

alternativeParts.reason: 13-bit differential ADC with SPI interface, cost-effective alternative
alternativeParts.useCase: Budget-conscious applications where 13-bit resolution is sufficient

companionParts.description: 5V ultra-low-noise LDO for clean analog power supply
3.3V LDO regulator for digital logic supply
RS-485 transceiver for industrial communication
ARM Cortex-M3 microcontroller for system control
Precision instrumentation amplifier for signal conditioning
4.096V precision voltage reference for external reference applications
ESD protection for analog inputs in harsh environments

faqs.question: What is the maximum input voltage for the ACM1604?
faqs.answer: The ACM1604 accepts differential input voltages up to +/- Vref. With the internal 4.096V reference, the maximum differential input is +/- 4.096V. The absolute maximum rating for analog inputs is -0.3V to AVDD + 0.3V. Exceeding these limits may cause permanent damage to the device. For applications requiring higher input voltages, use external resistor dividers or signal conditioning circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: For input voltages exceeding +/- 4.096V, implement external attenuation or contact our FAE team for alternative product recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I interface the ACM1604 with my microcontroller?
faqs.answer: The ACM1604 uses a standard SPI interface with CS (Chip Select), SCLK (Serial Clock), DIN (Data In), and DOUT (Data Out) pins. Connect CS to a GPIO pin for device selection, SCLK to the SPI clock, DIN to MOSI, and DOUT to MISO. The interface supports SPI modes 0 and 3 with clock rates up to 20 MHz. A typical conversion sequence involves: 1) Pull CS low to select the device, 2) Send configuration byte on DIN, 3) Read 16-bit conversion result on DOUT, 4) Pull CS high to end transaction. The device also supports daisy-chain mode for multi-channel systems using a single SPI bus.
faqs.decisionGuide: Ensure your microcontroller SPI supports mode 0 or 3. For multiple ADCs, consider using the daisy-chain feature to save GPIO pins.

faqs.question: What is the effective resolution at different sampling rates?
faqs.answer: The ACM1604 maintains 16-bit resolution across all sampling rates up to 1 MSPS. However, effective resolution (ENOB) varies with sampling rate due to noise considerations. At 1 MSPS, expect approximately 14.5 ENOB. At 500 kSPS, ENOB improves to about 15.0 bits. At 100 kSPS and below, you can achieve the full 16-bit ENOB. The internal programmable averaging filter can improve ENOB at the cost of reduced sampling rate. For highest precision measurements, operate at lower sampling rates or use oversampling techniques in software.
faqs.decisionGuide: For maximum precision, operate at 100 kSPS or below. For high-speed acquisition, 1 MSPS provides excellent performance with 14.5+ ENOB.

faqs.question: Can I use an external voltage reference with the ACM1604?
faqs.answer: Yes, the ACM1604 supports both internal and external voltage references. The internal reference provides 4.096V with 10 ppm/°C drift, suitable for most applications. For higher precision, you can connect an external reference to the REF pin. The external reference voltage can range from 2.5V to 5.0V, allowing you to optimize the input range for your specific application. When using an external reference, the input range scales proportionally (input range = +/- Vref). Ensure your external reference has low noise and good temperature stability for best results. Bypass the REF pin with a 10uF capacitor close to the device.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use internal reference for standard applications. Use external reference when specific voltage levels or higher precision is required.

faqs.question: What PCB layout considerations are important for the ACM1604?
faqs.answer: Proper PCB layout is essential for achieving datasheet performance. Key considerations include: 1) Place decoupling capacitors (100nF + 10uF) as close as possible to AVDD and DVDD pins, ideally within 2mm. 2) Use separate analog and digital ground planes connected at a single point near the ADC. 3) Keep analog input traces short and away from digital signals. 4) Use differential routing for analog input pairs with matched trace lengths. 5) Provide adequate copper area for thermal management. 6) Avoid routing switching power supplies under or near the ADC. 7) Use solid ground planes to minimize noise coupling. Following these guidelines ensures optimal performance and noise immunity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow the recommended layout in the datasheet. Contact our FAE team for layout review if experiencing noise issues.

faqs.question: How does the programmable gain amplifier (PGA) work?
faqs.answer: The ACM1604 includes an integrated PGA with programmable gains of 1x, 2x, 4x, and 8x. The PGA amplifies small input signals before conversion, improving the effective resolution for low-level signals. For example, with a 100mV input signal and 8x gain, the ADC sees 800mV, utilizing more of the converter's dynamic range. The PGA is configured through the SPI interface by setting the appropriate bits in the configuration register. Note that higher gain settings reduce the maximum allowable input voltage range proportionally. At 8x gain, the maximum differential input is +/- 0.512V (4.096V / 8). The PGA features excellent linearity and low drift, maintaining accuracy across all gain settings.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use PGA for small signals to maximize dynamic range. Ensure input signal times gain does not exceed reference voltage.

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Precision Analog
faeReview.content: In my 15 years supporting precision analog designs, I've found the ACM1604 to be an excellent choice for industrial data acquisition systems. The 1 MSPS sampling rate combined with true 16-bit resolution makes it versatile for both control loops and measurement applications. I particularly appreciate the integrated PGA - it eliminates the need for external amplifiers in many sensor interface designs, reducing BOM cost and board space. The 120 dB CMRR is genuinely impressive and makes a real difference in noisy industrial environments. I've deployed this ADC in motor control systems where it reliably rejected switching noise that plagued other converters. One recommendation: pay close attention to the reference voltage layout. Use a solid ground plane and keep the reference bypass capacitor within 3mm of the REF pin. I've seen systems achieve 15.5 ENOB with proper layout, while poor layout can degrade performance to 13 bits.
faeReview.highlight: Excellent balance of speed, resolution, and noise immunity for industrial applications

products.name: 24-Bit 4-Channel Sigma-Delta ADC
products.shortDescription: 24-bit sigma-delta ADC with 4 differential channels, PGA up to 128x, and 20 ENOB for precision measurement applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACM2420 is a high-resolution 24-bit sigma-delta analog-to-digital converter designed for precision measurement applications requiring exceptional accuracy and noise performance.

Featuring four differential input channels with a programmable gain amplifier (1x to 128x), the ACM2420 can resolve microvolt-level signals from sensors such as strain gauges, thermocouples, and RTDs.

With 20 effective bits of noise-free resolution at 10 SPS, integrated 50/60 Hz rejection, and low offset drift of 0.02 μV/°C, this ADC delivers laboratory-grade performance for industrial applications.

alternativeParts.reason: Similar 24-bit sigma-delta architecture with more input channels
alternativeParts.useCase: Applications requiring more than 4 input channels with similar precision
Higher channel count applications requiring fast sampling

companionParts.description: Precision instrumentation amplifier for bridge sensor conditioning
RTD signal conditioner with excitation and linearization
3.3V ultra-low-noise LDO for analog power supply
ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller with FPU for digital filtering
2.5V precision voltage reference for external reference mode
RS-485 transceiver for industrial communication
ESD protection for analog inputs in industrial environments

faqs.question: What is the effective resolution of the ACM2420 at different output data rates?
faqs.answer: The ACM2420's effective resolution (ENOB) varies with output data rate due to the sigma-delta architecture's noise shaping characteristics. At the lowest rate of 5 SPS, you achieve the full 20 ENOB with noise-free resolution of 20 bits. At 10 SPS, ENOB is approximately 19.5 bits. At 100 SPS, expect around 18 ENOB. At the maximum 2 kSPS rate, ENOB is approximately 16 bits. This trade-off between speed and resolution is fundamental to sigma-delta converters. For weigh scale applications requiring maximum precision, use 10-20 SPS. For temperature monitoring where speed is less critical, 5-10 SPS provides optimal results. The integrated digital filter provides excellent 50/60 Hz rejection at lower data rates, making this ADC ideal for power-line-connected equipment.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select output data rate based on your application's speed vs precision requirements. Use lower rates for maximum precision.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the noise-free resolution for my specific application?
faqs.answer: Noise-free resolution represents the number of bits that are stable (not toggling due to noise). To calculate: 1) Determine your peak-to-peak noise from the datasheet table based on your selected data rate and PGA gain. For example, at 10 SPS with 1x gain, peak-to-peak noise is approximately 2.5 μV. 2) Calculate the full-scale input range: for a 2.5V reference with 1x gain, FS = 5V (differential +/- 2.5V). 3) Calculate noise-free counts: FS / noise = 5V / 2.5μV = 2,000,000 counts. 4) Convert to bits: log2(2,000,000) = 20.9 bits. 5) Round down to get noise-free resolution: 20 bits. Higher PGA gains reduce the input range proportionally but can improve noise-free resolution for small signals by utilizing more of the ADC's dynamic range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use the highest PGA gain that accommodates your signal range to maximize noise-free resolution for small signals.

faqs.question: What is the best way to interface with RTD temperature sensors?
faqs.answer: For RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) measurement with the ACM2420, use a ratiometric measurement configuration for best accuracy. Connect the RTD in a 3-wire or 4-wire configuration to eliminate lead resistance errors. Use the ACM2420's internal excitation current source (100μA or 200μA) to drive the RTD. Connect the excitation voltage to the ADC reference input (REFIN) to implement ratiometric measurement - this cancels out excitation current variations. Set the PGA gain based on the expected RTD resistance range. For Pt100 RTDs (100Ω at 0°C), use 8x or 16x gain. For Pt1000 RTDs (1000Ω at 0°C), use 1x or 2x gain. Enable the 50/60 Hz filter to reject power line interference. With proper configuration, you can achieve +/- 0.1°C accuracy across the full -200°C to +850°C range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ratiometric measurement with internal excitation current for RTD applications. Select PGA gain based on RTD type and expected temperature range.

faqs.question: How do I minimize offset drift in precision measurements?
faqs.answer: Minimizing offset drift requires attention to both hardware and software techniques. Hardware considerations: 1) Use the internal reference voltage which has excellent temperature stability (10 ppm/°C). 2) Ensure good thermal design - keep heat sources away from the ADC and use copper pours for thermal distribution. 3) Use low-drift external components when needed. 4) Implement proper PCB layout with star grounding. Software techniques: 1) Perform offset calibration at startup by shorting inputs and measuring the offset. 2) Implement periodic offset calibration during operation if the system allows brief measurement interruptions. 3) Use chop mode if available, which automatically cancels offset through internal switching. 4) For thermocouple applications, ensure the cold junction compensation sensor is thermally coupled to the input terminals. The ACM2420's inherent offset drift of 0.02 μV/°C is excellent, but proper system design is still essential for maintaining accuracy over temperature.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement offset calibration at startup and consider periodic recalibration. Ensure good thermal design and use ratiometric measurement where possible.

faqs.question: Can the ACM2420 be used for multi-channel thermocouple measurement?
faqs.answer: Yes, the ACM2420 is excellent for multi-channel thermocouple measurement systems. Its four differential channels can monitor up to 4 thermocouples simultaneously. Key configuration points: 1) Set PGA gain to 32x or 64x to amplify the small thermocouple signals (typically 10-60 mV full scale). 2) Implement cold junction compensation (CJC) using an integrated temperature sensor or external sensor placed near the thermocouple connection terminals. 3) Enable the 50/60 Hz rejection filter to eliminate power line interference. 4) Use the burnout current sources to detect open thermocouple conditions. 5) For best results, use the ACM2420's single-cycle settling mode when switching between channels. The 24-bit resolution provides sufficient dynamic range to resolve 0.1°C temperature changes even with Type K thermocouples. For systems requiring more than 4 channels, multiple ACM2420 devices can share the same SPI bus using separate chip select lines.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use PGA gain of 32x-64x for thermocouples. Implement cold junction compensation and burnout detection for robust operation.

faqs.question: What are the key differences between the ACM2420 and the ACM1604?
faqs.answer: The ACM2420 and ACM1604 serve different application requirements despite both being AcelaMicro ADCs. The ACM2420 is a sigma-delta converter optimized for high-resolution, low-speed precision measurement with 24-bit resolution and 20 ENOB at low data rates. It's ideal for weigh scales, temperature sensors, and precision instrumentation. The ACM1604 is a SAR converter designed for higher speed applications with 16-bit resolution and 1 MSPS sampling rate, making it better for control loops and data acquisition systems. The ACM2420 features integrated 50/60 Hz rejection and programmable excitation currents for sensor applications. The ACM1604 offers faster channel switching and lower latency. For multi-channel weigh scale systems, choose the ACM2420. For motor control or vibration monitoring, choose the ACM1604. Both offer excellent industrial-grade reliability and automotive qualification options.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose ACM2420 for high-precision, low-speed measurement. Choose ACM1604 for moderate-precision, high-speed applications.

faeReview.author: Sarah Johnson
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Sensor Applications
faeReview.content: The ACM2420 is my go-to recommendation for precision measurement applications, especially weigh scales and temperature monitoring systems. The 24-bit resolution with 20 ENOB delivers laboratory-grade performance in an industrial-grade package. I've implemented this ADC in numerous weigh scale designs where it consistently achieves 1:10,000 resolution or better. The integrated PGA is a game-changer - being able to amplify microvolt-level signals without external amplifiers simplifies designs and reduces cost. The 50/60 Hz rejection is genuinely effective; I've seen it eliminate power line interference that plagued other converters. One critical design tip: the reference voltage is crucial for precision. Use the internal reference for most applications, but if you need external reference, choose a low-noise, low-drift reference like the REF5025. Also, pay attention to the input common-mode range - it must stay within AVDD - 1.0V. For thermocouple applications, this means you may need to bias the inputs above ground. Overall, this is an outstanding precision ADC that rivals products costing significantly more.
faeReview.highlight: Laboratory-grade precision with industrial reliability - exceptional value for precision measurement applications

categories.name: Power Management ICs
categories.description: AcelaMicro's power management portfolio includes high-efficiency DC-DC converters, low-dropout linear regulators (LDOs), and battery management solutions for industrial and automotive applications.
categories.longDescription: AcelaMicro power management IC distributor selection guide: Our comprehensive power management portfolio includes high-efficiency DC-DC buck and boost converters, ultra-low-noise LDOs, and battery management ICs. The ACM series power management products feature wide input voltage ranges, high efficiency, and excellent load transient response. These products are ideal for industrial automation, automotive electronics, medical devices, and portable applications. All products are available through LiTong Electronics with full technical support and competitive pricing.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select AcelaMicro Power Management ICs
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting the right DC-DC converter or LDO based on efficiency, noise, and thermal requirements.

faqs.question: How do I choose between a switching regulator and an LDO?
faqs.answer: Switching regulators like the ACM34063 provide high efficiency (85-95%) by storing energy in inductors, making them ideal for battery-powered applications and large input-output voltage differentials. However, they generate switching noise and require external inductors. LDOs like the ACM7805 offer simplicity, low noise, and fast transient response but dissipate excess power as heat. Choose switching regulators for efficiency-critical applications and LDOs for noise-sensitive analog circuits or small voltage drops.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use switching regulators for efficiency and large voltage drops. Use LDOs for low noise and small voltage differentials.

faqs.question: What efficiency can I expect from AcelaMicro DC-DC converters?
faqs.answer: AcelaMicro DC-DC converters achieve efficiencies of 85% to 95% depending on input voltage, output voltage, and load current. The ACM34063 synchronous buck converter achieves peak efficiency of 94% at moderate loads. Efficiency is typically highest at 50-80% of rated load current. At light loads, efficiency decreases due to quiescent current and switching losses. At heavy loads, conduction losses in the MOSFETs and inductor become significant. For battery-powered applications, consider the efficiency at your typical operating point rather than peak efficiency.
faqs.decisionGuide: Expect 85-95% efficiency. Check efficiency curves at your operating point for battery applications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power dissipation in an LDO?
faqs.answer: LDO power dissipation is calculated as Pd = (Vin - Vout) × Iout + (Vin × Iquiescent). The dominant term is (Vin - Vout) × Iout. For example, with Vin = 5V, Vout = 3.3V, and Iout = 200mA, dissipation is (5 - 3.3) × 0.2 = 0.34W. Calculate junction temperature as Tj = Ta + (Pd × θja). Ensure Tj remains below the maximum rating (typically 125°C or 150°C). For high dissipation applications, consider switching regulators or LDOs with thermal pads for better heat sinking.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate dissipation using Pd = (Vin - Vout) × Iout. Verify junction temperature is within limits.

faqs.question: What is PSRR and why is it important for LDOs?
faqs.answer: PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio) measures how well an LDO rejects noise and variations on its input from appearing at the output. It is expressed in dB, with higher values indicating better rejection. For example, a PSRR of 80 dB at 1 kHz means input noise at 1 kHz is attenuated by 10,000x. PSRR varies with frequency - typically high at low frequencies but decreasing at higher frequencies. For powering sensitive analog circuits like ADCs and RF components, high PSRR at the frequencies of interest is critical. The ACM7805 offers excellent PSRR of 80 dB at 1 kHz.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select LDOs with high PSRR at frequencies present in your application. Check PSRR curves, not just single points.

faqs.question: How do I select the right inductor for my DC-DC converter?
faqs.answer: Inductor selection involves balancing ripple current, size, and cost. Calculate minimum inductance using L = (Vout × (Vin - Vout)) / (Vin × fsw × ΔIL), where fsw is switching frequency and ΔIL is inductor ripple current (typically 20-40% of output current). Higher inductance reduces ripple current but increases size and cost. The inductor must have saturation current rating higher than the peak current (Iout + ΔIL/2). DCR (DC resistance) affects efficiency - lower DCR is better. Shielded inductors reduce EMI. For AcelaMicro's ACM34063 at 1A output, a 10μH inductor with 2A saturation current and <100mΩ DCR is typical.
faqs.decisionGuide: Size inductor for 20-40% ripple current. Ensure saturation current rating exceeds peak current.

products.name: 3A Synchronous Buck Converter
products.shortDescription: 3A synchronous buck converter with 4.5V-36V input, adjustable output, and 94% peak efficiency for industrial apps.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACM34063 is a high-efficiency synchronous step-down DC-DC converter capable of delivering up to 3A continuous output current.
With a wide input voltage range of 4.5V to 36V and adjustable output voltage down to 0.8V, this converter is ideal for industrial and automotive applications.
The synchronous rectification architecture achieves peak efficiency of 94%, reducing power loss and thermal requirements compared to non-synchronous solutions.

alternativeParts.reason: Similar non-synchronous buck converter with lower efficiency
alternativeParts.comparison: ACM34063=><LM2596: Input voltage 40V > 36V (higher), Output current 3A = 3A (same), Efficiency 80% < 94% (lower), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.useCase: Cost-sensitive applications where efficiency is less critical

alternativeParts.reason: Compact 3A buck converter with high frequency operation
alternativeParts.comparison: ACM34063=><MP1584: Input voltage 28V < 36V (lower), Output current 3A = 3A (same), Frequency 1.5MHz fixed vs adjustable, suitable for direct replacement

companionParts.description: 5V LDO for post-regulation of sensitive analog circuits
3.3V LDO for microcontroller supply
Power inductor for buck converter output filter
Schottky diode for bootstrap circuit
ADC for monitoring output voltage and current
22μF ceramic capacitor for input filtering
47μF ceramic capacitor for output filtering

faqs.question: What is the maximum output current of the ACM34063?
faqs.answer: The ACM34063 can deliver up to 3A continuous output current with proper thermal management. The actual maximum current depends on input voltage, output voltage, ambient temperature, and heatsinking. At high input-output voltage differentials, thermal limitations may reduce the maximum current. For example, with Vin = 24V, Vout = 5V, and Ta = 85°C, the maximum current may be limited to 2A due to power dissipation. Always verify thermal performance in your specific application using the thermal resistance specifications (θja = 40°C/W for TSSOP-16).
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for 3A with adequate heatsinking. Perform thermal calculations for your operating conditions.

faqs.question: How do I set the output voltage?
faqs.answer: The output voltage is set using a resistor divider from Vout to FB (feedback) pin to ground. The formula is Vout = 0.8V × (1 + R1/R2), where R1 is the top resistor (Vout to FB) and R2 is the bottom resistor (FB to GND). For example, for 5V output: R1 = 52.5kΩ, R2 = 10kΩ. Use 1% tolerance resistors for accurate output voltage. Place the resistors close to the FB pin to minimize noise pickup. The feedback pin has 0.8V reference voltage with 1% accuracy. For adjustable versions, output voltage can be set from 0.8V to 24V.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use resistor divider formula Vout = 0.8V × (1 + R1/R2). Place resistors close to FB pin.

faqs.question: What switching frequency should I use?
faqs.answer: Switching frequency selection involves trade-offs between efficiency, component size, and EMI. Lower frequencies (300-500kHz) provide higher efficiency but require larger inductors and capacitors. Higher frequencies (1-2MHz) allow smaller passive components but have lower efficiency due to increased switching losses. The ACM34063 supports 300kHz to 2.4MHz. For maximum efficiency, use 300-500kHz. For minimum size, use 1.5-2MHz. For automotive applications, consider fixed frequency operation below 2MHz to avoid AM radio interference. The frequency can be set with an external resistor or synchronized to an external clock.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 300-500kHz for efficiency, 1.5-2MHz for small size. Avoid AM band for automotive.

faqs.question: How do I minimize EMI from the switching regulator?
faqs.answer: Minimizing EMI requires attention to layout, filtering, and shielding. Keep the high-current switching loop (input cap, switch node, inductor) as small as possible. Place input capacitor close to Vin and GND pins. Use shielded inductors to contain magnetic fields. Add an RC snubber (10Ω + 100pF) across the switching node if needed. Implement a pi filter on the input for conducted EMI. Use a solid ground plane under the converter. Keep sensitive analog circuits away from the switching node. Follow the recommended PCB layout in the datasheet. For radiated EMI, ensure good ground continuity and minimize trace lengths.
faqs.decisionGuide: Keep switching loops small, use shielded inductors, add input filtering, follow reference layout.

faqs.question: Can the ACM34063 be used for negative output voltages?
faqs.answer: Yes, the ACM34063 can be configured as an inverting buck-boost converter to generate negative output voltages. In this configuration, the output is negative with respect to ground, and the IC ground pin is connected to the negative output. The maximum output current is reduced compared to buck configuration due to higher switch currents. For example, a -5V output at 1A from 12V input is achievable. Refer to the datasheet for the inverting configuration schematic. Note that the input voltage rating is reduced in inverting mode because the switch sees Vin + |Vout|. For high current negative outputs, consider using a dedicated inverting converter.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use inverting buck-boost configuration for negative outputs. Reduce current rating by 30-40%.

faqs.question: What protection features does the ACM34063 include?
faqs.answer: The ACM34063 includes comprehensive protection features: Overcurrent protection (OCP) limits peak switch current to 4.5A typical. Thermal shutdown disables the converter when die temperature exceeds 160°C, with 20°C hysteresis. Under-voltage lockout (UVLO) prevents operation when input voltage is below 4.0V. Soft-start limits inrush current during startup with 4ms ramp time. These protection features ensure reliable operation under fault conditions and prevent damage to the converter and load. The protection features are automatic and require no external components.
faqs.decisionGuide: Protection features are automatic. Ensure proper heatsinking to avoid thermal shutdown.

faeReview.author: David Park
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Power Electronics
faeReview.content: In my 18 years of power supply design, I've found the ACM34063 to be an excellent general-purpose buck converter for industrial applications. The wide 4.5V-36V input range covers most industrial and automotive scenarios, and the 94% peak efficiency is impressive for a converter in this price range. I particularly appreciate the adjustable switching frequency - being able to optimize for efficiency or size depending on the application is valuable. The internal compensation simplifies design and reduces component count. One important recommendation: pay close attention to the input capacitor selection. Use low-ESR ceramic capacitors (X5R or X7R) with adequate voltage rating. I've seen systems with insufficient input capacitance exhibit instability under load transients. For the inductor, I recommend shielded types to minimize EMI. The thermal performance is good with θja of 40°C/W, but for 3A operation at high ambient temperatures, consider adding copper area or a small heatsink. Overall, this is a reliable, cost-effective buck converter that I frequently recommend for industrial power supplies.
faeReview.highlight: Excellent efficiency and wide input range make it ideal for industrial power applications

products.name: 500mA Ultra-Low-Noise LDO
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low-noise 500mA LDO with 8μVrms noise, 80dB PSRR, and wide input range for precision analog applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACM7805 is a high-performance low-dropout linear regulator designed for noise-sensitive analog and RF applications.
With ultra-low output noise of 8μVrms and excellent PSRR of 80dB at 1kHz, this LDO provides clean power for precision ADCs, DACs, and RF circuits.
The device operates from 2.5V to 16V input and delivers up to 500mA output current with only 200mV dropout voltage at full load.

alternativeParts.comparison: ACM7805=><TPS7A49: Input voltage 36V > 16V (higher), Output current 150mA < 500mA (lower), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.useCase: Ultra-low-noise applications with lower current requirements

alternativeParts.comparison: ACM7805=><LD39130: Input voltage 5.5V < 16V (lower), Output current 300mA < 500mA (lower), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.useCase: Low-voltage applications with moderate current requirements

companionParts.description: Buck converter for pre-regulation to reduce LDO power dissipation
ADC powered by this LDO for clean analog supply
Ceramic capacitor for input stability
Low-ESR ceramic capacitor for output filtering
Ceramic capacitor for noise reduction
Precision ADC benefiting from low-noise supply
10kΩ resistor for enable pin if not used

faqs.question: What makes the ACM7805 suitable for precision analog circuits?
faqs.answer: The ACM7805 is specifically designed for noise-sensitive applications with several key features: Ultra-low output noise of 8μVrms (10Hz-100kHz) minimizes noise injection into analog circuits. High PSRR of 80dB at 1kHz effectively rejects input power supply noise. Excellent load and line regulation (±0.2% and ±0.1%) ensures stable output voltage under varying conditions. Low dropout voltage (200mV) allows operation with minimal headroom. These characteristics make it ideal for powering precision ADCs, DACs, RF circuits, and sensors where power supply noise directly impacts performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACM7805 for any noise-sensitive analog circuit requiring clean, stable power supply.

faqs.question: How much input-output voltage difference is needed for proper regulation?
faqs.answer: The ACM7805 requires a minimum dropout voltage of 200mV at 500mA load for proper regulation. At lighter loads, the dropout voltage is lower - approximately 100mV at 250mA. For reliable operation, maintain at least 300mV headroom (Vin - Vout) across all operating conditions including input voltage transients and temperature variations. For example, for 5V output, minimum input should be 5.3V. If your input voltage is only slightly higher than the desired output, consider using a lower dropout (LDO) version or a switching regulator for the pre-regulation stage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Maintain at least 300mV headroom for reliable regulation across all conditions.

faqs.question: What capacitors are recommended for the ACM7805?
faqs.answer: The ACM7805 requires specific capacitor types for stability and performance. Input capacitor: Use 10μF ceramic (X5R or X7R) close to the input pin to improve transient response and prevent oscillation. Output capacitor: Use 4.7μF ceramic with ESR < 1Ω for stability. Larger values (10μF) improve transient response. Bypass capacitor: Optional 0.1μF from BYP pin to ground reduces output noise. All capacitors should have voltage ratings at least 50% higher than the operating voltage. Place capacitors as close as possible to the respective pins with short trace lengths. Avoid tantalum capacitors as they have higher ESR.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 10μF ceramic on input, 4.7μF ceramic on output. Place close to pins.

faqs.question: How do I calculate thermal performance for the ACM7805?
faqs.answer: Thermal calculation is critical for LDOs due to power dissipation. Calculate power dissipation: Pd = (Vin - Vout) × Iout + (Vin × Iq). For example: Vin=9V, Vout=5V, Iout=300mA: Pd = (9-5) × 0.3 + (9 × 0.0012) = 1.2 + 0.011 = 1.21W. Calculate junction temperature: Tj = Ta + (Pd × θja). For SOT-23-5, θja = 150°C/W. At Ta=50°C: Tj = 50 + (1.21 × 150) = 231°C (exceeds max 125°C). This configuration requires heat sinking or reduced current. Solutions: reduce input voltage, reduce output current, add copper area for heat sinking, or use a switching pre-regulator to reduce dropout voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always perform thermal calculations. Add copper area or reduce Vin-Vout for high current.

faqs.question: Can the enable pin be used for sequencing multiple LDOs?
faqs.answer: Yes, the ENABLE pin can be used for power sequencing and management. The ENABLE pin is active high with 1.2V threshold. Connect to input voltage for always-on operation, or to a logic signal for on/off control. For sequencing multiple LDOs, connect the output of one LDO (through a voltage divider if needed) to the ENABLE of the next LDO. This creates a power-up sequence where downstream regulators turn on after upstream regulators are stable. The ENABLE pin has 1μA pull-down current, so a 10kΩ pull-up resistor is recommended when driven by open-drain signals. Enable switching time is approximately 100μs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ENABLE pin for power sequencing. Connect upstream output to downstream ENABLE.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the ACM7805 and a standard 7805?
faqs.answer: The ACM7805 is a modern CMOS LDO with significant advantages over legacy 7805 linear regulators: 1) Much lower dropout voltage: 200mV vs 2V typical, allowing operation with lower headroom. 2) Lower quiescent current: 1.2mA vs 5mA, better for battery applications. 3) Much lower output noise: 8μV vs hundreds of μV, critical for precision analog. 4) Higher PSRR: 80dB vs 60dB, better noise rejection. 5) Smaller package: SOT-23-5 vs TO-220. 6) Enable pin for power management. The main trade-off is lower maximum current (500mA vs 1A) and lower maximum input voltage (16V vs 35V). For modern designs, the ACM7805 offers superior performance in a smaller footprint.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACM7805 for new designs requiring low noise and low dropout. Use standard 7805 for high current (>500mA) or high voltage (>16V).

faeReview.author: Jennifer Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Analog Power
faeReview.content: The ACM7805 is my first choice for powering precision analog circuits. The 8μV noise floor is exceptional - I've measured it myself and it truly delivers on the datasheet spec. In one recent design for a medical device, we were struggling with ADC noise issues using a standard LDO. Switching to the ACM7805 reduced the noise floor by 15dB, allowing us to achieve the required 18-bit effective resolution. The PSRR is also genuinely excellent - I've seen it maintain 70dB+ even at 100kHz, which is critical for rejecting switching noise from upstream DC-DC converters. One important tip: the output capacitor selection matters more than you might think. While the datasheet specifies 4.7μF minimum, I recommend 10μF X7R ceramic for best transient response. Also, pay attention to the PCB layout - keep the output capacitor ground connection close to the LDO ground pin, not routed through the general ground plane. This minimizes ground bounce during load transients. For thermal management, remember that SOT-23-5 has limited heat dissipation capability. At 200mW+ dissipation, consider adding copper area or moving to a larger package. Overall, this is an outstanding LDO that competes with products costing 3x more.
faeReview.highlight: Exceptional noise performance and PSRR make it ideal for precision analog applications

categories.name: Interface ICs
categories.description: AcelaMicro's interface IC portfolio includes RS-485, CAN, and isolated transceivers for robust industrial communication.
categories.longDescription: AcelaMicro interface IC distributor selection guide: Our comprehensive interface portfolio includes RS-485 transceivers for multi-drop networks, isolated CAN transceivers for high-voltage safety, and digital isolators for signal isolation. These products feature high ESD protection, wide operating temperature ranges, and excellent common-mode transient immunity (CMTI). Ideal for industrial automation, automotive, and building automation applications.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select AcelaMicro Interface ICs
selectionGuide.description: Guide for selecting the right transceiver based on protocol, isolation, and environmental requirements.

faqs.question: When should I use isolated vs non-isolated transceivers?
faqs.answer: Use isolated transceivers when there is a need to break ground loops, provide safety isolation, or protect against high common-mode voltages. Isolation is essential in industrial environments with long cable runs, motor drives, or high-voltage systems. Non-isolated transceivers are suitable for short distances within the same ground domain where cost is a primary concern.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use isolated transceivers for long distances, high voltages, or ground loop prevention.

faqs.question: What is CMTI and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: CMTI (Common-Mode Transient Immunity) measures how well an isolated transceiver rejects fast voltage transients between its input and output sides. High CMTI (typically >25kV/μs) is essential in applications with switching power supplies, motor drives, or high-voltage inverters where fast transients can cause data errors. AcelaMicro isolated transceivers offer CMTI of 50kV/μs or higher.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select transceivers with CMTI >25kV/μs for noisy environments with switching equipment.

faqs.question: What is the maximum cable length for RS-485 networks?
faqs.answer: RS-485 maximum cable length depends on data rate. At 100kbps, maximum length is approximately 1200 meters. At 1Mbps, maximum length reduces to about 100 meters. At 20Mbps (ACM485 maximum), maximum length is approximately 15 meters. These limits are due to signal attenuation and timing considerations. Use twisted pair cable with characteristic impedance of 120Ω. Terminate both ends of the bus with 120Ω resistors to prevent reflections. For long distances, use proper cable shielding and grounding to minimize EMI.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 1200m max at low speeds, 15m max at 20Mbps. Always use twisted pair with termination.

faqs.question: How do I protect my CAN bus from ESD and transients?
faqs.answer: CAN bus protection requires multiple layers. Use transceivers with integrated ESD protection like ACM1050 (±8kV contact, ±15kV air). Add external TVS diodes on the CANH and CANL lines for additional surge protection. Use common mode chokes to filter high-frequency noise. Implement proper termination with split termination (two 60Ω resistors with capacitor to ground) for better EMI performance. Ensure proper shielding and grounding of the cable. For severe environments, consider isolated transceivers to protect against ground potential differences.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use integrated ESD protection, add TVS diodes, use common mode chokes, proper termination.

faqs.question: What is the difference between RS-485 and CAN bus?
faqs.answer: RS-485 is a physical layer standard defining electrical characteristics for multi-drop communication. It supports various protocols (Modbus, Profibus) and allows master-slave or multi-master configurations. Data rates up to 20Mbps are possible. CAN bus includes both physical layer and protocol layer (CAN 2.0B), with built-in arbitration, error detection, and fault confinement. CAN is ideal for real-time control systems with deterministic latency. RS-485 is simpler and lower cost for basic multi-drop communication. CAN is more robust for critical control applications. Both use differential signaling for noise immunity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use RS-485 for simple multi-drop communication. Use CAN for real-time control with error detection.

products.name: RS-485 Transceiver
products.shortDescription: Half-duplex RS-485 transceiver with 20Mbps data rate, 256 node capability, and ±15kV ESD protection.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACM485 is a robust half-duplex RS-485 transceiver designed for industrial communication networks.
Supporting data rates up to 20Mbps and up to 256 nodes on a single bus, this transceiver is ideal for multi-drop applications.
Integrated ±15kV ESD protection and fail-safe receiver ensure reliable operation in harsh industrial environments.

companionParts.description: 120Ω termination for bus ends
Additional surge protection

faqs.question: How many nodes can be connected to an RS-485 bus?
faqs.answer: The ACM485 supports up to 256 nodes on a single RS-485 bus. This is achieved through 1/8 unit load receiver input impedance (96kΩ). Standard RS-485 transceivers support 32 nodes (1 unit load). The maximum bus length is 1200 meters at lower data rates. For 20Mbps operation, maximum cable length is approximately 15 meters.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACM485 for high node count applications up to 256 devices.

faeReview.author: Robert Chen
faeReview.title: FAE - Industrial Communication
faeReview.content: The ACM485 is a solid RS-485 transceiver that I frequently recommend for industrial applications. The 20Mbps speed and 256-node capability are genuine advantages over older devices. The integrated ESD protection saves board space and cost. I've deployed this in numerous Modbus RTU networks with excellent reliability.
faeReview.highlight: High speed and high node count make it ideal for modern industrial networks

products.name: Isolated CAN Transceiver
products.shortDescription: Isolated CAN transceiver with 5000Vrms isolation, 1Mbps data rate, and 50kV/μs CMTI for automotive and industrial.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACM1050 is a galvanically isolated CAN transceiver providing robust communication in high-voltage environments.
With 5000Vrms isolation and 50kV/μs CMTI, this transceiver protects low-voltage control systems from high-voltage domains.
The device supports CAN 2.0B protocol up to 1Mbps and includes protection against bus faults and ESD.

companionParts.description: EMI filtering for CAN bus
60Ω + 60Ω with center tap capacitor

faqs.question: What level of isolation does the ACM1050 provide?
faqs.answer: The ACM1050 provides reinforced isolation rated at 5000Vrms per UL1577. This level of isolation is suitable for protection against electric shock in systems with working voltages up to 1000V. The device also has 50kV/μs CMTI for immunity to fast transients.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACM1050 for applications requiring safety isolation up to 5000Vrms.

faeReview.author: Michael Wang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Automotive Systems
faeReview.content: The ACM1050 is my go-to isolated CAN transceiver for EV and industrial applications. The 5000Vrms isolation and high CMTI are critical for BMS and motor drive applications. The integrated isolated power eliminates the need for a separate isolated DC-DC converter, saving significant BOM cost and board space.
faeReview.highlight: Integrated isolation power and high CMTI make it ideal for EV applications

categories.name: Sensor Interface
categories.description: AcelaMicro's sensor interface portfolio includes instrumentation amplifiers, RTD conditioners, and signal conditioning solutions.
categories.longDescription: AcelaMicro sensor interface distributor selection guide: Our sensor interface portfolio includes precision instrumentation amplifiers for bridge sensors, RTD signal conditioners with excitation and linearization, and multi-channel sensor AFEs. These products feature low offset, low noise, and high CMRR for accurate sensor measurement in industrial environments.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select AcelaMicro Sensor Interface ICs
selectionGuide.description: Guide for selecting the right signal conditioning solution for your sensor type.

faqs.question: What is an instrumentation amplifier and when do I need one?
faqs.answer: An instrumentation amplifier (INA) is a differential amplifier with high input impedance and excellent CMRR, designed for amplifying small differential signals from sensors like strain gauges and load cells. You need an INA when measuring small differential signals (mV range) in the presence of large common-mode voltages or noise. The ACM8221 provides 120dB CMRR and programmable gain from 1 to 1000.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use an INA for amplifying small differential sensor signals in noisy environments.

faqs.question: How do I condition an RTD temperature sensor?
faqs.answer: RTD conditioning requires excitation current, lead wire compensation, and linearization. The ACM5320 integrates all these functions. It provides programmable excitation current (100μA or 200μA), supports 2/3/4-wire configurations for lead resistance compensation, and includes internal linearization tables for Pt100 and Pt1000 RTDs. The ratiometric measurement architecture eliminates errors from excitation current variations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACM5320 for complete RTD signal conditioning with integrated linearization.

faqs.question: What is CMRR and why is it important for sensor applications?
faqs.answer: CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) measures how well an amplifier rejects signals that appear equally on both inputs (common-mode signals) while amplifying the difference between inputs (differential signals). High CMRR is critical for sensor applications because sensor cables often pick up noise (power line interference, EMI) that appears as common-mode signals. The ACM8221 offers 120dB CMRR, meaning common-mode noise is rejected by a factor of 1,000,000. This allows accurate measurement of small sensor signals (mV level) even in noisy industrial environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select amplifiers with CMRR >100dB for industrial sensor applications with long cables.

faqs.question: How do I choose the right gain for my instrumentation amplifier?
faqs.answer: Gain selection balances signal amplitude with bandwidth and noise. Calculate required gain as: Gain = Desired Output Range / Sensor Output Range. For example, a load cell with 2mV/V sensitivity at 5V excitation produces 10mV full scale. To utilize a 5V ADC range, need Gain = 5V / 10mV = 500. Consider noise: higher gain amplifies noise along with signal. Consider bandwidth: gain-bandwidth product is constant, so higher gain reduces bandwidth. The ACM8221 provides gain from 1 to 1000. Use the lowest gain that provides adequate signal amplitude for your ADC.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate gain based on sensor output and ADC range. Use lowest gain that provides adequate signal.

faqs.question: What is the difference between 2-wire, 3-wire, and 4-wire RTD connections?
faqs.answer: 2-wire RTD is simplest but includes lead wire resistance in measurement, causing errors. Use only for short leads where resistance is negligible. 3-wire RTD is most common - uses three wires of equal length and gauge. The measurement circuit compensates for lead resistance by measuring voltage drop in one lead and subtracting it. 4-wire RTD (Kelvin connection) is most accurate - uses separate pairs for excitation current and voltage measurement, completely eliminating lead resistance effects. The ACM5320 supports all three configurations, with 4-wire providing best accuracy for precision applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 2-wire for short leads only. Use 3-wire for most applications. Use 4-wire for highest accuracy.

products.name: Precision Instrumentation Amplifier
products.shortDescription: Precision instrumentation amplifier with 120dB CMRR, 1-1000 gain range, and 50μV max offset for sensor applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACM8221 is a precision instrumentation amplifier designed for sensor signal conditioning applications.
With 120dB CMRR, 50μV max offset voltage, and programmable gain from 1 to 1000, this amplifier accurately amplifies small differential signals.
Low noise of 25nV/√Hz and wide bandwidth of 1MHz make it suitable for high-resolution sensor systems.

companionParts.description: ADC for digitizing amplified sensor signal
Anti-aliasing filter before ADC

faqs.question: How do I set the gain of the ACM8221?
faqs.answer: The gain is set by a single external resistor RG connected between the RG pins. The formula is Gain = 1 + (49.4kΩ / RG). For example, for Gain = 100, RG = 499Ω. For Gain = 1000, RG = 49.4Ω. Use 0.1% tolerance resistors for accurate gain. The gain can be changed dynamically by switching different resistor values.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use precision resistors (0.1%) for accurate gain setting.

faeReview.author: Sarah Johnson
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Sensor Applications
faeReview.content: The ACM8221 is an excellent instrumentation amplifier that I frequently use for weigh scale and strain gauge applications. The 120dB CMRR is genuine - I've measured it myself. The low offset voltage means less calibration is needed. One tip: use a good quality gain resistor (0.1% or better) as the gain accuracy depends on it.
faeReview.highlight: High CMRR and low offset make it ideal for precision sensor applications

products.name: RTD Signal Conditioner
products.shortDescription: Complete RTD signal conditioner with excitation, lead wire compensation, and linearization for Pt100/Pt1000.
descriptionParagraphs: The ACM5320 is a complete signal conditioning solution for RTD temperature sensors.
The device integrates programmable excitation current, lead wire compensation for 2/3/4-wire RTDs, and automatic linearization.
Ratiometric measurement architecture eliminates errors from excitation current variations, providing accurate temperature measurement.

companionParts.description: ADC for digitizing conditioned RTD signal
Pt100 or Pt1000 temperature sensor

faqs.question: What RTD types are supported by the ACM5320?
faqs.answer: The ACM5320 supports Pt100 and Pt1000 RTDs with automatic linearization for both types. The device includes internal lookup tables for the Callendar-Van Dusen equation, providing linearization accuracy of ±0.1°C across the full -200°C to +850°C range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACM5320 for Pt100 or Pt1000 RTDs requiring high accuracy.

faeReview.author: Sarah Johnson
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Sensor Applications
faeReview.content: The ACM5320 is my go-to solution for RTD conditioning. It eliminates the need for external excitation circuits and linearization lookup tables. The ratiometric measurement is key - it cancels out excitation current drift. I've used this in many industrial temperature control systems with excellent results.
faeReview.highlight: Complete integrated solution simplifies RTD measurement design
